verses.commentary: Herod the tetrarch (Antipas — ruler of Galilee, son of Herod the Great). News of Jesus reaches the royal court. The Messiah cannot stay hidden forever.
Herod's guilty conscience: 'This is John the Baptist risen from the dead.' Resurrection imagined as guilt-recurrence. Herod fears his own crime more than he understands the Messiah.
Flashback to John's arrest. Bound and imprisoned over Herodias — wife of Herod's brother Philip whom Herod had taken.
John had been telling Herod 'it is not lawful' (ouk exestin, οὐκ ἔξεστιν — Levitical violation, Lev 18:16; 20:21). Prophetic confrontation with royal sin. John applied Torah to power.
Herod wants John dead but fears the crowd, which considers John a prophet. Political calculation restrains personal vengeance — temporarily.
Birthday banquet. Daughter of Herodias dances. Royal entertainment of doubtful propriety.
Herod sworn oath — whatever she asks. Drunk and impressed, he commits before he hears the request.
Prompted by her mother: John the Baptist's head on a platter. Herodias's calculated revenge weaponizes her daughter's dance.
King distressed (lypetheis, λυπηθείς) but unwilling to lose face. Public oath outweighs justice. Cowardice masked as honor.
Beheaded in prison. The greatest born of women (11:11) dies in obscurity for a dinner-party whim. The world cannot accommodate prophetic witness.
Head delivered on platter. The grotesque image — the symbol of Jewish honor presented as banquet trophy. John's voice silenced as evidence.
John's disciples take the body, bury, and report to Jesus. The forerunner's death is the first explicit hint of where Jesus' road leads.
Jesus withdraws (anachoreo again — strategic retreat) to a desolate place alone. Grief and threat both signal time apart. Crowds follow on foot.
Sees the crowd, has compassion (esplagchnisthe, ἐσπλαγχνίσθη), heals the sick. Even in grief, mercy operates. Compassion is reflex, not effort.
Disciples: 'Send them away to buy food.' Pragmatic solution. The crowd's hunger is real; the disciples' resources are not.
'You give them something to eat.' The impossible command activates dependence. Jesus delegates what only he can supply.
Five loaves, two fish. The famous accounting of inadequacy. Disciples present the negligible inventory.
'Bring them here to me.' Inadequacy placed in Jesus' hands becomes adequacy. Provisions surrender point is the multiplication point.

Crowds sit on grass (echo of Psalm 23 — the Shepherd makes lie down in green pastures). Jesus takes, blesses, breaks, gives — fourfold eucharistic verbs that will recur at the Last Supper (26:26). The Davidic shepherd feeds the flock.
All ate and were satisfied (chortazo, ἐχορτάσθησαν — same word as Beatitude 5:6). Twelve baskets left — one per disciple, one per tribe. Surplus, not scarcity.
Five thousand men, besides women and children. Total likely twenty thousand or more. Mass eucharistic foreshadowing.
Disciples sent ahead by boat. Jesus stays to dismiss the crowd. After Davidic shepherding, the dispersal — to prevent kingship-by-force (compare John 6:15).
Up the mountain to pray. Alone. The pattern returns: heavy ministry followed by solitary prayer.
Boat far from land, contrary wind. Disciples struggling. Their leader has stepped off the stage.
Fourth watch of the night (3-6 a.m.). Jesus walks on the sea — Yahweh-prerogative (Job 9:8; Psalm 77:19). Lord of chaos walks on chaos.
Disciples terrified — 'It's a phantom (phantasma, φάντασμα).' Fear distorts perception. They see ghost where there is God.
'Take heart, it is I (ego eimi, ἐγώ εἰμι), do not be afraid.' The ego eimi echoes YHWH's self-identification (Exodus 3:14). Theophany on the lake.
Peter: 'Lord, if it is you, command me to come.' Faith leans into the offer. Peter alone of the Twelve attempts to imitate the Lord's walk.
'Come.' Peter steps out, walks on water toward Jesus. The miracle reaches the disciple briefly.
Sees the wind, fears, begins to sink. 'Lord, save me!' (kyrie, soson me, Κύριε, σῶσόν με) — three desperate words. Faith's collapse exposes the prior achievement as borrowed.
Immediately Jesus catches him. 'You of little faith, why did you doubt?' (eis ti edistasas, εἰς τί ἐδίστασας — 'into what did you split your mind?'). Doubt as bifurcation, not as opposition.
Both into the boat. Wind ceases. Storm submits to the Lord's presence.
Those in the boat worship him — 'Truly you are the Son of God.' First confession by the Twelve corporately. The sea-rescue catalyzes Christological recognition.
Cross to Gennesaret. Geography moves the ministry along.
Men of the place recognize him, send word everywhere, bring the sick. Faith from regional locals contrasts with the Pharisees' calculated hostility.

They beg to touch the fringe of his cloak (kraspedon, κράσπεδον — tassel, same word as bleeding woman 9:20). All who touch are healed. The fringe theology repeats: the Servant's holiness contaminates outward into healing.
